subject: Chemistry
q:
The volume occupied by 4.75 g of acetylene gas at 50°C and 740 mm Hg pressure is ____________ L. (Rounded off to the nearest integer)

[Given R=0.0826 L atm K-1 mol-1]
question:
The volume occupied by 4.75 g of acetylene gas at 50°C and 740 mm Hg pressure is ____________ L. (Rounded off to the nearest integer)

[Given R=0.0826 L atm K-1 mol-1]
solution:
Given mass=4.75 g⇒C2H2g

⇒Moles=4.7526 mol

Temperature=50+273=323 K

P=740760atm

R=0.0826ℓ atmmol K

⇒V=nRTP=4.7526×0.0826×323740760

⇒V=96314.07819240=5.0059ℓ≃5ℓ

subject: Chemistry
q: Among the following, the incorrect statement is:
question: Among the following, the incorrect statement is:
options: at low pressure, real gases show ideal behaviour.
at very low temperature, real gases show ideal behaviour.
at Boyle's temperature, real gases show ideal behaviour.
at very large volume, real gases show ideal behaviour.
solution: A real gas behaves like ideal gas at high temperature and low pressure. At very low temperature intermolecular forces of attraction will be quite significant as molecular randomness is very low due to which at very low temperature real gases can't show ideal behaviour.

solution:
Vmp=2RTM ∝TM

For H2 at 300 K,   Vmp∝TM ∝3002=12.24

Vmp of N2<Ump of O2<Ump of H2

subject: Chemistry
q:
The pressure of a moist gas at 27°C is 4 atm. The volume of the container is doubled at the same temperature. The new pressure of the moist gas is …×10-1 atm. (Nearest integer)
(Given: The vapour pressure of water at 27°C is 0.4 atm)
question:
The pressure of a moist gas at 27°C is 4 atm. The volume of the container is doubled at the same temperature. The new pressure of the moist gas is …×10-1 atm. (Nearest integer)
(Given: The vapour pressure of water at 27°C is 0.4 atm)
solution:
Dalton's Law states that the total pressure P of moist air is the sum of partial pressures of dry air Pa and water vapor Pv: P= Pa+Pv. Only the absolute pressure is the state parameter of moist air.

Pgas+PH2O=4

Pgas=4-0.4=3.6 atm

As volume is doubled, Pgasnew =1.8 atm

New Total Pressure =1.8+0.4=2.2 atm

q:
Arrange the following gases in increasing order of van der Waals constant 'a'
(A) Ar
(B) CH4
(C) H2O
(D) C6H6
Choose the correct option from the following.
question:
Arrange the following gases in increasing order of van der Waals constant 'a'
(A) Ar
(B) CH4
(C) H2O
(D) C6H6
Choose the correct option from the following.
solution:
The van der Waals' constant ′a′ is directly proportional to the intermolecular force of attraction; hence, the gas with greater value of  ′a′ can be most easily liquefied.The values of Vander Waal's constant 'a' for the given gases are:
    Gases                    Vander Waal's Constant 'a'
A. Ar                           1.35 L2 atm mol2
B. CH4                       2.25L2 atm  mol2
C. H2O                       5.536L2 atm mol2
D. C6H6                       18.57L2 atm mol2
∴ Increasing order of 'a' for the given gases
A<B<C<D

subject: Chemistry
q:
For 1 mol of gas, the plot of pV vs p is shown below. p is the pressure and V is the volume of the gas.

What is the value of compressibility factor at point A?
question:
For 1 mol of gas, the plot of pV vs p is shown below. p is the pressure and V is the volume of the gas.

What is the value of compressibility factor at point A?
solution:
According to Van der Waals equation,

(P + aVm2) (Vm - b) = RT

Where, Vm = molar volume of the gas

R = Universal gas constant

T = Temperature

P = Pressure

At point A 'a' is considerable and 'b' is negligible.

So, (P + aVm2) Vm = RT

PVm + aVm = RT

Compressibility factor (Z) = 1 - aVmRT

Hence, the correct answer is 1 - aRTV

subject: Chemistry
q:
At the sea level, the dry air mass percentage composition is given as nitrogen gas : 70.0, oxygen gas : 27.0 and argon gas : 3.0. If total pressure is 1.15 atm , then calculate the ratio of followings respectively :
(i) partial pressure of nitrogen gas to partial pressure of oxygen gas
(ii) partial pressure of oxygen gas to partial pressure of argon gas
(Given : Molar mass of $\mathrm{N}, \mathrm{O}$ and Ar are 14, 16, and $40 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ respectively)
question:
At the sea level, the dry air mass percentage composition is given as nitrogen gas : 70.0, oxygen gas : 27.0 and argon gas : 3.0. If total pressure is 1.15 atm , then calculate the ratio of followings respectively :
(i) partial pressure of nitrogen gas to partial pressure of oxygen gas
(ii) partial pressure of oxygen gas to partial pressure of argon gas
(Given : Molar mass of $\mathrm{N}, \mathrm{O}$ and Ar are 14, 16, and $40 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ respectively)
solution:
$\frac{\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{N}_2}}{\mathrm{Po}_2}=\frac{\mathrm{x}_{\mathrm{N}_2} \cdot \mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{T}}}{\mathrm{x}_{\mathrm{O}_2} \cdot \mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{T}}}=\frac{\mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{N}_2}}{\mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{O}_2}}$ {using Dalton's law of partial pressure}
$\begin{aligned} & =\frac{70 / 28}{27 / 32}=2.96 \\ & \frac{\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{O}_2}}{\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{Ar}}}=\frac{\mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{O}_2}}{\mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{Ar}}}=\frac{27 / 32}{3 / 40}=11.25\end{aligned}$

subject: Chemistry
q:
Which one of the following graphs is not correct for ideal gas?

d= Density, P= Pressure, T=Temperature
question:
Which one of the following graphs is not correct for ideal gas?

d= Density, P= Pressure, T=Temperature
solution:
For ideal gas

PM = dRT

d=PMR1T

Graph between density versus 1/T should be straight line.

Graph between density versus P should be straight line.

So graph between d Vs T is not straight line.

Hence option II is wrong.

q:
Geraniol, a volatile organic compound, is a component of rose oil. The density of the vapour is 0.46gL-1 at 257 °C and 100 mmHg. The molar mass of geraniol is (Nearest Integer)
[Given R=0.082 L atm K-1mol-1]
question:
Geraniol, a volatile organic compound, is a component of rose oil. The density of the vapour is 0.46gL-1 at 257 °C and 100 mmHg. The molar mass of geraniol is (Nearest Integer)
[Given R=0.082 L atm K-1mol-1]
solution:
Assuming ideal behaviour P=dRTM

P=100760 atm, T=257+273=530 K

d=0.46gm/L

So M=0.46×0.082×530100×760

=151.93≈152

subject: Chemistry
q:
An empty LPG cylinder weighs 14.8 kg. When full, it weighs 29.0 kg and shows a pressure of 3.47 atm. In the course of use at ambient temperature, the mass of the cylinder is reduced to 23.0 kg. The final pressure inside the cylinder is _____ atm. (Nearest integer)

(Assume LPG to be an ideal gas)
question:
An empty LPG cylinder weighs 14.8 kg. When full, it weighs 29.0 kg and shows a pressure of 3.47 atm. In the course of use at ambient temperature, the mass of the cylinder is reduced to 23.0 kg. The final pressure inside the cylinder is _____ atm. (Nearest integer)

(Assume LPG to be an ideal gas)
solution:
Initial mass of gas =29-14.8=14.2Kg

mass of gas used : =29-23=6Kg

gas left =14.2-6=8.2Kg

(1) 3.47×V=14.2×103M×R×T

(2) p×V=8.2×103M×R×T

Divide : (1)/(2)⇒  3.47P=14.28.2

P=2.003

q: For a reaction, $\mathrm{N}_2 \mathrm{O}_{5(\mathrm{~g})} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{NO}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}$ in a constant volume container, no products were present initially. The final pressure of the system when $50 \%$ of reaction gets completed is
question: For a reaction, $\mathrm{N}_2 \mathrm{O}_{5(\mathrm{~g})} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{NO}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}$ in a constant volume container, no products were present initially. The final pressure of the system when $50 \%$ of reaction gets completed is
solution: $\begin{gathered}x=\frac{P_0}{2} \\ P_{\text {total }}=P_0-\frac{P_0}{2}+P_0+\frac{P_0}{4}=\frac{7}{4} P_0\end{gathered}$

subject: Chemistry
q:
Given below are two statements. One is labelled as Assertion A and the other is labelled as Reason R.

Assertion A : Activated charcoal adsorbs SO2 more efficiently than CH4.

Reason R : Gases with lower critical temperatures are readily adsorbed by activated charcoal. In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below.
question:
Given below are two statements. One is labelled as Assertion A and the other is labelled as Reason R.

Assertion A : Activated charcoal adsorbs SO2 more efficiently than CH4.

Reason R : Gases with lower critical temperatures are readily adsorbed by activated charcoal. In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below.
options: Both <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi></math> are correct and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi></math> is the correct explanation of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math>.

Both <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi></math> are correct but <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi></math> is NOT the correct explanation of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math>.

<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> is correct but <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi></math> is not correct.

<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> is not correct but <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi></math> is correct.
solution:
Lesser the value of critical temperature of gases lesser will be the extent of adsorption.

Gas SO2 CH4
Critical temperature/K 630 190

The critical temperature depends on the strength of the intermolecular interactions that hold a substance together as a liquid.

SO2 has stronger intermolecular force of attractions than CH4, due to the more molar mass and non-zero dipole moment.

So, Activated charcoal adsorbs SO2 more efficiently than CH4. is correct statement but Reason statement is not correct.

subject: Chemistry
q:
Van der Waal's equation for a gas is stated as,

P=nRTV-nb-anV2.

This equation reduces to the perfect gas equation, P=nRTV When,
question:
Van der Waal's equation for a gas is stated as,

P=nRTV-nb-anV2.

This equation reduces to the perfect gas equation, P=nRTV When,
options: temperature is sufficiently high and pressure is low.
both temperature and pressure are very low.
both temperature and pressure are very high.
temperature is sufficiently low and pressure is high.
solution:
At very high temperatures & low pressures, the characteristic constant behaviour can be understood from virial expansion

Z = PV m RT = 1 + b - a RT 1 V m + b 2 V m 2 + ....

Vm, molar volume large i.e  b 2 V m 2 → o

i.e Third term in virial expansion can be ignored.

In qualitative terms, the real gas will show ideal behaviour when the intermolecular forces of attraction will be negligible which is possible at high temperature and low pressure.
Also  b - a RT → o

i.e.  Z ≈ 1  and gas behaves ideally.

subject: Chemistry
q: The unit of the van der Waals gas equation parameter 'a' in P+an2V2(V-nb)=nRT is:
question: The unit of the van der Waals gas equation parameter 'a' in P+an2V2(V-nb)=nRT is:
solution:
Pressure correction ΔP=an2V2

a=ΔP×V2n2atm(Lit)2( mole )2=atmdm32( mole )2

a=atm·dm6·mol-2

q: At <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>300</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></math>, a sample of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of gas A occupies the same volume as <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of hydrogen at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>200</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></math> at the same pressure. The molar mass of gas <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>mol</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. (nearest integer) Assume that the behaviour of gases as ideal. (Given: The molar mass of hydrogen <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></mfenced></math> gas is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi>mol</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>.)
question: At <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>300</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></math>, a sample of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of gas A occupies the same volume as <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of hydrogen at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>200</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></math> at the same pressure. The molar mass of gas <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>mol</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. (nearest integer) Assume that the behaviour of gases as ideal. (Given: The molar mass of hydrogen <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></mfenced></math> gas is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi>mol</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>.)
solution:
PV=nRTV=nRTPVH2=VA (Given)

nRTPA=nRTPH2

3×300MA=0.22×200

Hence, the molar mass of A, MA=45g

q: <math><mn>0.5</mn></math> moles of gas <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi></math> moles of gas <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math> exert a pressure of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>200</mn><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">P</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi></math> in a container of volume <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>3</mn></msup></math> at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1000</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></math> . Given, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi></math> is the gas constant in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>JK</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><msup><mi>mol</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi></math> is:
question: <math><mn>0.5</mn></math> moles of gas <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi></math> moles of gas <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math> exert a pressure of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>200</mn><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">P</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi></math> in a container of volume <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>3</mn></msup></math> at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1000</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></math> . Given, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi></math> is the gas constant in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>JK</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><msup><mi>mol</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi></math> is:
solution:
According to the ideal gas equation:

PV=nRT

200×10=(0.5+x)R×1000

2=0.5R+xR

x=2-0.5RR=4-R2R

subject: Chemistry
q: A mixture of hydrogen and oxygen contains <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>40</mn><mo>%</mo></math> hydrogen by mass when the pressure is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2</mn></math> bar. The partial pressure of hydrogen is bar.
question: A mixture of hydrogen and oxygen contains <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>40</mn><mo>%</mo></math> hydrogen by mass when the pressure is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2</mn></math> bar. The partial pressure of hydrogen is bar.
solution:
According to Dalton's law of partial pressures, the total pressure by a mixture of gases is equal to the sum of the partial pressures of each of the constituent gases. 

Pi=xiPPi= partial pressure of ith component.xi=mole fraction of the ith componentP = total pressure

Let WH2=40 g⇒nH2=402=20

WO2=60 g⇒nO2=6032=158

PH2=2020+158×2.2

=2020+1.875×2.2

=2021.875×2.2

=2.0114

≃2.01bar

subject: Chemistry
q: When does a gas deviate the most from its ideal behavior?
question: When does a gas deviate the most from its ideal behavior?
options: At low pressure and high temperature
At high pressure and high temperature
At low pressure and low temperature
At high pressure and low temperature
solution:
At high pressure & low temperature gas almost near liquefaction range with

(i) - Intense intermolecular forces

(ii) - Volume occupied by molecules is substantial due to low intermolecular distances.

(Round off to the Nearest Integer). [Assume chlorine is an ideal gas at STP <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>083</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">L</mi><mo> </mo><mi>bar</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>mol</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math><math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>,</mo><msub><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>6</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>023</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>23</mn></msup><mo>]</mo></math>
question: The number of chlorine atoms in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mL</mi></math> of chlorine gas at STP is _______ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>21</mn></msup></math>. (Round off to the Nearest Integer). [Assume chlorine is an ideal gas at STP <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>083</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">L</mi><mo> </mo><mi>bar</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>mol</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math><math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>,</mo><msub><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>6</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>023</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>23</mn></msup><mo>]</mo></math>
solution:
PV=nRT

1.0×201000=N6.023×1023×0.083×273

∴ Number of Cl2 molecules, N=5.3×1020

Hence, Number of Cl -atoms =1.06×1021

≈1×1021

subject: Chemistry
q:
The number of statement's, which are correct with respect to the compression of carbon dioxide from point (a) in the Andrews isotherm from the following is _________.

A. Carbon dioxide remains as a gas upto point (b)
B. Liquid carbon dioxide appears at point (c)
C. Liquid and gaseous carbon dioxide coexist between points (b) and (c)
D. As the volume decreases from (b) to (c), the amount of liquid decreases
question:
The number of statement's, which are correct with respect to the compression of carbon dioxide from point (a) in the Andrews isotherm from the following is _________.

A. Carbon dioxide remains as a gas upto point (b)
B. Liquid carbon dioxide appears at point (c)
C. Liquid and gaseous carbon dioxide coexist between points (b) and (c)
D. As the volume decreases from (b) to (c), the amount of liquid decreases
solution:
Andrews noticed that at high temperatures isotherms look like that of an ideal gas and the gas cannot be liquefied even at very high pressure. As the temperature is lowered, shape of the curve changes and data shows considerable deviation from ideal behaviour.

Carbon dioxide remains as a gas only up to point b. 

At point b, liquid of a particular volume appears. Further compression does not change the pressure. Liquid and gaseous carbon dioxide coexist and further application of pressure results in the condensation of more gas until the point c is reached. At point c, all the gas has been condensed and further application of pressure merely compresses the liquid as shown by steep line. A point like d represents liquid state
